title: Nonclassical Cutoff Fluctuations in Squeezed-Light-Driven High-Harmonic Generation
authors: Tsendsuren Khurelbaatar, R. T. Sang, Igor Litvinyuk
abstract: Strong-field high-harmonic generation (HHG) is conventionally described semiclassically, with the driving laser treated as a classical field. This approximation becomes insufficient in nanoscale interaction geometries, where extreme spatial confinement raises the vacuum-field amplitude to the ~10^-2 level relative to the driving-field amplitude. When the quantum fluctuations of the driving field are redistributed between conjugate quadratures by squeezing, they can be directly imprinted onto macroscopic HHG observables. To model this interaction, we employ a Wigner phase-space approach that maps the quantum-optical driver onto a stochastic ensemble of time-dependent Schrodinger equation realizations. Although each realization remains classically simulable, the sub-vacuum quadrature covariance structure of squeezed states cannot be reproduced by any field admitting a non-negative Glauber-Sudarshan P-representation. Within this single-mode Gaussian framework, we show that amplitude squeezing suppresses the shot-to-shot variance of the HHG cutoff below the standard quantum limit (SQL). To leading order in the vacuum-to-driver ratio, the normalized cutoff variance decays exponentially with the squeezing parameter, independent of the absolute vacuum-field amplitude and therefore robust against uncertainties in the effective nanoscale mode volume. A subleading phase-noise contribution from the anti-squeezed quadrature produces a variance minimum near r_opt ~ 1.6, providing a concrete experimental target. These results establish the HHG cutoff variance ratio as a nonlinear, self-calibrating Heisenberg witness in which sub-SQL scaling directly reflects the redistribution of quantum uncertainty in the driving field.
summary: This paper investigates how squeezed light can be used to control and certify quantum fluctuations in high-harmonic generation. It identifies a specific observable—the variance of the HHG cutoff—that acts as a self-calibrating witness for nonclassicality, even in uncertain nanoscale environments. The study reveals that amplitude squeezing can push these fluctuations below the standard quantum limit.
main_problem: Determining how quantum fluctuations from squeezed-light drivers manifest in macroscopic high-harmonic generation (HHG) observables and identifying a robust 'Heisenberg witness' to certify nonclassicality in nanoscale geometries.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that amplitude squeezing suppresses the shot-to-shot variance of the HHG cutoff below the standard quantum limit, providing a self-calibrating witness that is independent of the absolute vacuum-field amplitude.
method: A Wigner phase-space approach is used to map the quantum-optical driver onto a stochastic ensemble of time-dependent Schrodinger equation (TDSE) realizations, supplemented by analytical cumulant expansions and ADK theory.
model_or_system: A 1D model atom (Argon) driven by a single-mode Gaussian squeezed coherent state in a nanoscale interaction regime where vacuum-field amplitude is significantly elevated.
key_observables: HHG cutoff position, HHG cutoff variance, and the normalized cutoff variance ratio relative to the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL).
important_parameters: Squeezing parameter (r), squeezing angle (theta), vacuum-to-driver amplitude ratio (~10^-2), and the Keldysh parameter (gamma < 0.5).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to a single-mode Gaussian framework and assumes an undepleted driving field and coherent phase locking.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Wigner distributions and time-domain field realizations for coherent, amplitude-squeezed, and phase-squeezed states; Figure 4 illustrates the dependence of the mean cutoff and variance on the squeezing angle and parameter.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantum-to-classical mapping in HHG, describes the Wigner-based stochastic TDSE method, presents analytical derivations for cutoff fluctuations, and concludes with the identification of a robust Heisenberg witness.
why_interesting: It bridges quantum optics and strong-field physics by showing how quadrature-specific noise redistribution can be mapped onto macroscopic spectral observables, offering a way to detect nonclassicality without complex homodyne detection.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Wigner phase-space representation and stochastic field realizations. Top row: Wigner distributions for (a) a coherent state (CS), (b) an amplitude-squeezed state (AS, r = 1), and (c) a phase-squeezed state (PS, r = 1). Teal points are quadrature pairs (Xi, Pi) sampled from the corresponding Wigner distribution; solid black ellipses are the 1σ contours, and dashed crimson circles indicate the SQL. Bottom row: time-domain field realizations E(t) for (d) coherent, (e) amplitude-squeezed, and (f) phase-squeezed pulses. Thin teal lines show individual stochastic realizations; the solid black line is the mean field. In (f), a rare high-amplitude realization (highlighted in orange)...

FIG. 2. Statistical filtering of tunnel ionization. (a) Ion- ization contribution P(I)Γ(I) versus normalized intensity I/I0 for CS (black dashed), AS (blue), and PS (red shaded). The bare ADK rate Γ(I) (gray dotted, right axis) illustrates ex- ponential weighting toward rare high-intensity realizations. (b) Normalized ionization yield ⟨Γ⟩/Γcoh versus r, comparing numerical evaluation of Eq. (12) for AS (blue dashed) and PS (red dashed) with the analytical approximation Eq. (15) (filled symbols).

FIG. 3. HHG cutoff extension under squeezed-field driving. (a) Ensemble-averaged HHG spectra for CS (black), AS (blue), and PS (red) drivers at r = 2.5. The classical cutoff Ip + 3.17Up(E0) (gray dashed) and the analytical stochastic prediction Eq. (18) (red dotted, PS Theory) are indicated. (b) Extracted cutoff position versus r from TDSE simulations (filled circles) and from the analytical prediction Eq. (18) (red solid) for the phase-squeezed (PS) case, with the coherent- state SQL reference (gray dashed).

FIG. 4. Quadrature-resolved HHG cutoff statistics. (a) Mean cutoff ⟨H⟩versus squeezing angle θ at fixed r = 1.5 for the squeezed state (blue circles) and the classical P ≥0 benchmark (dark red squares). The CS SQL reference is shown as a gray dashed line. (b) Mean cutoff versus r under amplitude squeezing (θ = 0) for AS (blue circles), the CS SQL reference (gray dashed), and the analytical prediction from Eq. (18) (red dotted curve). The exact TDSE diverges upward from the analytical model at extreme squeezing due to higher-order phase noise contributions. (c) HHG cutoff variance ∆H2 versus θ.
